coredata.dc:title: Phosphorus-doped carbon dots as an effective flame retardant for transparent PVA composite films with enhanced UV shielding property
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Runying
Yi, Xindan
Liu, Xinyu
Wang, Haojie
Wang, Lingzhi
Zeng, Birong
Chen, Guorong
Xu, Yiting
Yuan, Conghui
Dai, Lizong
coredata.dc:description: In order to endow polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film with the functionalities of highly-effective flame retardancy and optical properties, the strategy of doping phosphorus into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and then using the P-containing CQDs as flame retardant is worthy investigating. In this work, m-phenylenediamine (MPD), 2-carboxyl ethyl(phenyl)phosphinic acid (HPP) and phytanic acid (PA) were used as carbon source and phosphorus sources to synthesize three kinds of CQDs, which were named as MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs respectively. Subsequently, the CQDs-PVA composite films were prepared by blending CQDs with PVA solution through casting process, the flame retardancy and UV shielding properties of the PVA-CQDs composite films were tested by kinds of techniques such as vertical burning tests (UL-94), limited oxygen index (LOI), UV–visible spectroscopy and so on. In addition, the residual char structure of the composite films was investigated by SEM and EDS. The results showed that compared with phosphorus-free MPD-CQDs, the two P-containing CQDs, both MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA CQDs, produced better flame-retardant effect on the PVA film. As the percentage of phosphorus increased, the flame retardancy of CQDs-PVA composite film greatly enhanced. When 15% MPD-HPP-CQDs and 15% MPD-PA-CQDs were added to PVA, the LOI value of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA was 27.8% and 31.5% respectively, meanwhile, the UL-94 rating reached VTM-1 and VTM-0, respectively. Due to the intact layer of P-containing condensed phase, the gas diffusion and oxygen transportation were hindered, which slowed down the combustion and promoted the further carbonation. In addition, both MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films exhibited good UV shielding properties without affecting the transparency of PVA itself.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PVA film
Carbon dots
Flame retardancy
UV-resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fabrication of PVA-CQDs composite film
Structural and optical properties of CQDs
Transparency and light shielding performance of PVA-CQDs composite films
Mechanical properties of PVA-CQDs composite films
Thermal stability of PVA-CQDs composite films
Flame retardancy of PVA-CQDs composite films
Residual char analysis of PVA-CQDs composite films
Gas product analysis of PVA-CQDs composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, size distribution, FTIR and XRD spectra of MPD-CQDs (a - d), MPD-HPP-CQDs (a' - d'), and MPD-PA-CQDs (a'′ - d'′).

UV–vis absorption, FL excitation and emission spectra of (a) MPD, (b) MPD-HPP-CQDs and (c) MPD-PA-CQDs; the inset photograph was the fluorescent luminescence photograph under 365 nm UV lamp. FL emission spectra of (a') MPD, (b') MPD-HPP-CQDs and (c') MPD-PA-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XPS (c) XRD spectra of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.
(a) UV–vis transmittance spectra, (b) optical band gap, and (c) optical pictures of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.
The SEM images of fracture surface of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films: (a) PVA; (b) MPD-CQDs-PVA; (c) 15%MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA; (d) 15%MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA; (e) Stress-strain curves.
(a) TGA and (b) DTG curves of the PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films under N2 atmosphere, (c and d) under air atmosphere.
UL-94 vertical burning tests of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.
HRR and THR curves of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.

SEM and EDS images of residual char of out-layer (a-d) and inner-layer (a'-d'). (a, a') PVA; (b, b') MPD-CQDs-PVA; (c, c') 15%MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA; (d, d') 15%MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA; (e) EDS mapping of 15% MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and (f) EDS mapping of 15%MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA.
Raman spectra of the residual char of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.
3D TG-FTIR spectra of (a) MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA, (c) MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA; FTIR spectra of pyrolysis products at different temperatures of (b) MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and (d) MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA.
Tensile data of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.
The TGA results of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.
LOI values, UL-94 vertical combustion rating, and MCC data of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films.

head.ce:title: Phosphorus-doped carbon dots as an effective flame retardant for transparent PVA composite films with enhanced UV shielding property

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to endow polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film with the functionalities of highly-effective flame retardancy and optical properties, the strategy of doping phosphorus into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and then using the P-containing CQDs as flame retardant is worthy investigating. In this work, m-phenylenediamine (MPD), 2-carboxyl ethyl(phenyl)phosphinic acid (HPP) and phytanic acid (PA) were used as carbon source and phosphorus sources to synthesize three kinds of CQDs, which were named as MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs respectively. Subsequently, the CQDs-PVA composite films were prepared by blending CQDs with PVA solution through casting process, the flame retardancy and UV shielding properties of the PVA-CQDs composite films were tested by kinds of techniques such as vertical burning tests (UL-94), limited oxygen index (LOI), UV–visible spectroscopy and so on. In addition, the residual char structure of the composite films was investigated by SEM and EDS. The results showed that compared with phosphorus-free MPD-CQDs, the two P-containing CQDs, both MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA CQDs, produced better flame-retardant effect on the PVA film. As the percentage of phosphorus increased, the flame retardancy of CQDs-PVA composite film greatly enhanced. When 15% MPD-HPP-CQDs and 15% MPD-PA-CQDs were added to PVA, the LOI value of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA was 27.8% and 31.5% respectively, meanwhile, the UL-94 rating reached VTM-1 and VTM-0, respectively. Due to the intact layer of P-containing condensed phase, the gas diffusion and oxygen transportation were hindered, which slowed down the combustion and promoted the further carbonation. In addition, both MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films exhibited good UV shielding properties without affecting the transparency of PVA itself.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Two phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots endowed polyvinyl alcohol film with both highly-effective flame retardancy and excellent UV and blue-light shielding properties without affecting the transparency of PVA itself.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is highly water soluble and hydrophilic due to the presence of a large number of hydroxyl groups in its backbone. It also has good biodegradability, compatibility, processability and thermal properties, therefore, PVA has become one of bio-based and renewable polymer, and has been widely used in many fields such as biomedicine, packaging, food processing, adhesives and construction and so on [1–4]. However, PVA is flammable with a limited oxygen index value (LOI) of 19.5%. When PVA is ignited, its flame propagation varies with its shape and thickness [5]. Typically, the initial and maximum decomposition temperature of PVA is about 200 °C and 300 °C, respectively. Once PVA is exposed to an ignition source, its polymer chain will break rapidly to form small molecules, resulting in severe burning out and melt dripping with very few residual char [6,7]. At present, the way to improve the flame retardancy of PVA becomes an important research topic.

Adding phosphorus flame retardants to PVA is one of the effective ways to reduce the flammability of PVA [8]. Existing reports have shown that the flame-retardant mechanism of phosphorus flame retardants usually depends on their oxidation state in flame retardants. In the high oxidation state (+3, +5), phosphorus flame retardants exhibit a condensed phase mechanism, which promotes polymer dehydration and char formation through the decomposition of phosphorus compounds (e.g., phosphoric acid and polyphosphates) to form a dense char layer that protects the underlying polymer from heat transfer [9]. The free radicals generated during the decomposition of phosphorus flame retardants in the low oxidation state (0, +1) can trap H∙ and HO∙, thus interrupting the combustion reaction and exhibiting a gas-phase mechanism. Typical phosphorus-containing flame retardants, such as ammonium polyphosphate [10] and melamine polyphosphate [11], usually were used at a relatively high addition amount. It might cause the problem of poor dispersion, agglomeration or precipitation behavior, which not only reduces flame retardant efficiency and mechanical properties, but also further affects transparency of PVA film.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a kind of novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a particle size <10 nm. CQDs have a variety of surface functional groups and internal carbon skeleton structures, which make them easy to be surface functionalized and complexed with inorganic, organic, polymer, and biologically active substances, and provide unique enhancement effects on matrix materials [12]. Fluorescent CQDs can be doped with nitrogen, phosphorus and other flame retardant elements, showing potential flame retardant applications [13,14]. Phosphorus-doped carbon dots not only have the advantages of low toxicity [15], good biocompatibility [16], tunable fluorescence [17], but also can effectively improve the flame retardant properties of the polymer matrix [18]. This is because under high-temperature thermal radiation, phosphorus element as an acid source can promote the material surface dehydration into carbon, inhibiting the thermal decomposition and combustion of the matrix under high-temperature conditions. Moreover, CQDs were considered to be potential additives for polymer films with UV shielding because of the ability to absorb and scatter UV radiation [19–22]. Therefore, CQDs have indeed emerged as a novel material for enhancing the flame-retardant properties while endowing the PVA film materials with special functions such as UV/blue light protection.

The strategy of doping phosphorus into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is expected to produce novel flame retardant for polymers. However, the phosphorus source in the synthesis of new CQDs would affect the flame retardancy efficiency. Besides, the formation of new hybrid materials with multi-functionalities is worthy more investigations. In this work, in order to explore the impact of phosphorus compounds on the flame retardancy of PVA at a relative low addition (≤15%), and to obtain a PVA composite film with other functions such as good optical properties, we designed one phosphorus-free CQDs using m-phenylenediamine as carbon source and two phosphorus-doped CQDs using 2-carboxyl ethyl(phenyl)phosphinic acid (HPP, percentage of element P is 14%) and phytanic acid (PA, percentage of element P is 28.2%) as phosphorus source for a comparison. The three CQDs (MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs) were further added to PVA to prepare composite films. The optical, mechanical and flame-retardant properties were characterized, and the effect of phosphorus content on the flame-retardant properties of PVA was investigated.

m-Phenylenediamine (MPD), Phytic acid (PA), 2-carboxyl ethyl (phenyl) phosphinic acid (HPP) and Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, PR China). Ultrapure water was provided by a P10Y water system.

The MPD-CQDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, the weighted 0.75 g MPD was added to ultrapure water at a concentration of 0.05 g/mL, after stirring and ultrasonication until complete dissolution, the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL), and then heated at 200 °C for 16 h. After cooling to room temperature, the products were then centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was further filtrated by ultra-filtration membrane (0.22 μm) to remove impurities. After that, the liquid was dialyzed against water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut off = 3500 Da for 24 h to remove residual reagents. MPD-HPP-CQDs was synthesized as that for MPD-CQDs, The MPD and HPP were added to the hydrothermal reactor at the mass ratio of 2:1 and reacted at 200 °C for 16 h. Similarly, MPD-PA-CQDs was synthesized as that for MPD-HPP-CQDs, just replacing HPP with PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of PVA-CQDs composite film
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of PVA was added to deionized water and mixed with 0.1 g/mL CQDs solution after ultrasound. PVA was configured into a 10% solution, which was stirred at 95 °C for 2 h, poured into a 0.12 mm thick glass mold, stood overnight, dried at 60 °C for 6 h, and 0.12 mm thick CQDs composite film was obtained. In this paper, the weight ratio of CQDs relative to PVA is 5%, 10%, and 15% respectively, named X-MPD-CQDs-PVA, X-MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and X-MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA, where X is the weight ratio. Finally, the dried membranes were stored in a vacuum box for further studies.

The Fourier infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) test mode was ATR, and the wavenumbers range was 500–4000 cm−1. The fluorescence spectrometer adopted wavelength scanning mode (excitation spectrum and emission spectrum) with a scanning speed of 240 nm/min and a voltage of 700 V. Ultraviolet-visible Spectrometer (UV–vis) test wavelengths range from 200 to 800 nm. Field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM), Talos F200s, with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The test conditions of X-ray diffractometer (XRD) were set as 40 kV voltage, 40 mA current, 5° ∼ 90° scanning angle range. The mechanical properties of the CQDs composite films were tested by microcomputer-controlled electronic universal testing machine CMT6503. The sample size was 50 mm × 5 mm × 0.10 mm, the tensile rate is 50 mm/min. The thermal properties of CQDs composites film were investigated by DSC and TGA. The film sample size for the LOI experiment was 140 mm × 52 mm × 0.10 mm according to ASTM D2863–17, and the experimental data were obtained by averaging the five films tested. The dimensions of the vertical burning tests (UL-94) were 200 mm × 50 mm × 0.10 mm according to ASTM D4804–14. The heat release rate of the films was measured on a microcalorimeter (MCC) by heating 5–6 mg of the sample from room temperature to 750 °C at a heating rate of 1 °C/s. The elementary composition of CQDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were used to observe the compatibility of CQDs and PVA matrix, as well as the inner and outer layer morphology with accelerating voltage was 10 kV. Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) were used to analysis element distribution of residual char. Analysis of gas-phase products after combustion of composite membranes by TG-FTIR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and sizes of MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs were characterized using TEM (Fig. 1a, a', a'′). It could be seen that the three spherical CQDs have similar sizes with a uniformly dispersion. The lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of about 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) planar lattice of graphitic carbon [23]. As shown in Fig. 1(b, b', b'′), the particle sizes of MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs ranged from 1.0 to 3.0 nm, with an average size of about 1.9 nm. The chemical groups of the CQDs were analyzed by FTIR. In the FTIR spectrum of m-phenylenediamine (MPD), the absorption peaks at 3397 cm−1, 3327 cm−1 and 1624 cm−1 were assigned to the asymmetric stretching vibration, stretching vibration and bending vibration of -NH2, and the absorption peaks at 1560 cm−1 and 1276 cm−1 were attributed to the CC and CN bonds respectively. It could be seen in the IR spectrum of MPD-CQDs that these characteristic absorption peaks also appeared but had broadened shape (Fig. 1c), implying MPD-CQDs possessed the groups of -NH2, CC and CN. In the FTIR spectrum of MPD-HPP-CQDs (Fig. 1c'), the peaks at 1143 cm−1 and 1003 cm−1 were observed, which was attributed to PO bond and P-O-R respectively [24]. In addition, the peak of CO at 1735 cm−1 in HPP disappeared while a new characteristic peak at 1670 cm−1 appeared, indicating the formation of -NHCOO- due to the reaction between MPD and HPP. Compared with MPD, the absorption peaks of MPD-PA-CQDs were also broadened and, and the peaks of PO bond and P-O-R also appeared in Fig. 1c'′. Therefore, it could be concluded that the surfaces of MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs, and MPD-PA-CQDs were rich in -NH2 functional groups, and the surfaces of MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs also contained phosphorus-containing functional groups, which would contribute to the flame retardancy of PVA composite films. The XRD spectra Fig. 1(d-d'′) showed that a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 15–35° was observed for MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs, which was attributed to the disordered graphite structure, corresponding to the (002) crystalline plane of the graphitic carbon [25].

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs were characterized. Fig. 2 (a-c) showed that one main absorption peak at 345 nm was observed for the three CQDs, which corresponded to the π-π* electron transition of the benzene ring. In addition, another absorption peak at 445 nm was observed for MPD-HPP-CQDs, which was attributed to the π-π* electron transition of CO [26]. The FL emission spectra showed that the maximum excitation wavelength of MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs were 340 nm, 385 nm, and 385 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, the maximum emission wavelength of MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs were 420 nm, 470 nm and 480 nm, respectively. The inset images presented that MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs emitted strong blue light under the irradiation of UV lamp at 365 nm. Fig. 2(a'-c') showed that MPD-CQDs, MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs had obvious excitation wavelength dependence. As the excitation wavelengths increased, the corresponding FL emission peak of the three CQDs varied in intensity and exhibited red-shift.

The FL emission spectra of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films at 365 nm excitation wavelength were shown in Fig. S1, from which it could be seen that the pure PVA films had no fluorescence property. As for the MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA composite film (Fig. S1a), the PVA film with 5% MPD-HPP-CQDs appeared a broad emission band at 425 nm and showed dual emission properties. The MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA composite films showed a typical excitation wavelength dependence similar to the MPD-HPP-CQDs aqueous solution, i.e., the emission wavelength was gradually red-shifted from 415 nm to 455 nm when the excitation wavelength of PVA composite films was increased from 330 nm to 400 nm (Fig. S1b). The optimal excitation wavelength of the MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA film was 380 nm, which was blue-shifted compared to the MPD-HPP-CQDs itself. This might be explained by the reason that the PVA molecules would passivate the surface of the CQDs through the hydrogen bonding interaction, and then produce short-wavelength emission [27]. The fluorescence properties of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films were similar to those of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA composite films. As can be seen from Fig. S1c, the emission peaks of the MPD-PA-CQDs-HPP composite film gradually broaden with the increase of the CQDs content, showing the multi-emission characteristic and red-shift phenomenon. Fig. S1d showed the fluorescence emission behavior of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite film under different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength ranged from 325 to 405 nm, the emission wavelength ranged from 411 nm to 463 nm, accompanying with the variation of the fluorescence intensity. The optimal excitation wavelength of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite film was 365 nm. Compared to MPD-PA-CQDs, the difference in the excitation wavelength might be attributed to the hydrogen bonding interaction between the CQDs and PVA.

The FTIR spectra of pure PVA, MPD-CQDs-PVA, MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films were shown in Fig. 3a. It could be seen that the pure PVA film had a significant absorption peak at 3266 cm−1, which was assigned to -OH group. When CQDs were added into PVA, the absorption peak of -OH varied in shape and shift, indicating that the presence of hydrogen bond interaction which originated from the -NH2/-P-OH in CQDs interacted with the -OH in PVA [28]. Compared pure PVA film with composite films, new absorption peak at 1608 cm−1 appeared in the CQDs-PVA composite films, which corresponded to the double bond CC bond in CQDs. Besides, when MPD-HPP-CQDs or MPD-PA-CQDs were added to PVA, the composite films had additional 1603 cm−1 (C=O), 1086 cm−1 (P=O) and 1048 cm−1 (P-OH) absorption peaks. These results indicated the successful preparation of PVA-CQDs composite films. XPS characterization was adopted to investigate the chemical composition of the PVA composite film, and the XPS data were shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S2. It could be found that three predominant peaks at 285, 399 and 532 eV assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s were observed for MPD-CQDs-PVA, MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA, indicating the N-doping for all the three composite films. For C1s resolved peaks of MPD-CQDs-PVA, there were two peaks at 284.8 and 286.3 eV, ascribing to C-C/C=C/C-H and CN respectively. Two resolved peaks in the N1s spectrum of MPD-CQDs-PVA at 399.3 and 400.6 eV were attributable to the NH and CN groups, respectively. In addition, the P2p peaks were observed for MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA. In the P2p spectrum of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA, the two peaks at 134.1 eV and 133.9 eV were belong to P-O-C and PO, respectively. MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA also had the peaks of PO and PO in 133.8 and 134.7 eV in the P2p spectrum. From the O1s spectrum of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA, it was seen that two divided peaks at 532.4 eV and 534.1 eV were assigned to PO and C-O-C/C-OH, respectively. Therefore, the XPS results confirmed the results of the FTIR analysis. Fig. 3c showed the XRD patterns of several PVA films. The pure PVA film had a prominent diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.1°, which corresponded to the diffraction peak of the crystalline region (101) of PVA [29], and the MPD-CQDs-PVA, MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA also had the typical PVA peaks without obvious changes, which implied that the added CQDs had good compatibility with PVA and did not damage the crystalline phase of PVA-CQDs composite films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transparency and light shielding performance of PVA-CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis transmittance spectra were recorded to characterize the light shielding properties of PVA and PVA-CQDs composite films. As shown in Fig. 4a, the transmittance of pure PVA film in the ultraviolet (UV-A) and blue light region (400–450 nm) was about 90%, which indicated that pure PVA film almost had no shielding on these lights. When MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs were added into PVA film, the UV transmittance greatly reduced for PVA-CQDs films. With the increase of CQDs content, the transmittance at UV-A and high-energy blue light (HEBL, band range of 380–500 nm) decreased gradually, indicating the occurrence of light-shielding effect. When 15%MPD-HPP-CQDs were added, the transmittance at 360 nm and 465 nm were 40% and 64%, respectively. When 15% MPD-PA-CQDs were added, the transmittance at 360 nm and 465 nm were only 14.37% and 58.29%, respectively. Therefore, MPD-PA-CQDs had better light shielding effect on PVA than MPD-HPP-CQDs. However, in the region of 500–760 nm, the transmittance of both MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films were still higher than 80%, indicating that the addition of MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs did not damage the transparency of PVA composite films. The optical pictures of PVA-CQDs composite films with different CQDs addition ratios (Fig. 4c) showed a good transparency which proved the results in Fig. 4a. In order to further estimate the optical bandgap (Eg) of PVA-CQDs composite films, the following eq. (1) [30,31] was applied based on the data obtained from UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S3).
(1)
αhν
2
= k
hν − Eg

In the equation, α was the absorption coefficient, hν was the energy of the incident light photon, k was the constant of proportionality, and Eg was the intersection of the extrapolated straight line and the horizontal coordinate. It was seen in Fig. S3a that two absorption peaks at 360 nm and 465 nm appeared in MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA. The absorption intensities increased with the increase of the content of MPD-HPP-CQDs, which coincided with the peaks in Fig. 4a. The absorption peaks of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA (Fig. S3b) were also in consistent with the UV transmission bands. According to eq. (1), the plot curve of (hv)2 versus hv was shown in Fig. 4b. The highest Eg was calculated to be 4.23 eV for pure PVA and 3.94 eV for MPD-CQDs-PVA. When the addition of MPD-HPP-CQDs was 5%, 10%, and 15%, the Eg of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA composite films were 3.92 eV, 3.87 eV, and 3.83 eV, respectively. When the addition of MPD-PA-CQDs was 5%, 10%, and 15%, the Eg of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films were 3.90 eV, 3.85 eV, and 3.79 eV, respectively. Obviously the MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films exhibited tunable optical bandgap properties, and the Eg decreased as the concentration of CQDs increased. It was known that the Eg depended on the energy level difference between the LUMO and HOMO levels. Lower energy level of LUMO or higher energy level of HOMO decreased the Eg [32]. Combined with the structural characterization of MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs, it was concluded that the two CQDs contained strong electronegative CO, which exhibited electron-withdrawing property, leading to the lowering of the LUMO energy level, and thus contributing to the lowering of Eg. Compared to the MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA film, the MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA film with a same CQDs content had a smaller Eg. Therefore, MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite film had better UV shielding performance than MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA composite film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of PVA-CQDs composite films
Thermal stability of PVA-CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The TGA and DTG characterizations of PVA and composite films were presented in Fig. 6 and Table 2 . The results showed that the thermal decomposition process of PVA composite films were divided into two stages in N2 atmosphere and three stages in air atmosphere (Fig. 6a, c). The first stage was the degradation of the PVA main chains within the range of 200–390 °C, producing a large amount of water and flammable small molecules. The second stage occurred at 390–500 °C, corresponding to the decomposition of cross-linking regions. In air atmosphere the deep oxidation and cyclization of molecules formed the third stage higher than 500 °C. It was seen from Fig. 6a that pure PVA film mainly degraded in the first stage, with a mass loss of about 83% in N2. The maximum decomposition temperature at first stage Tmax1 was about 304.9 °C with a maximum thermal weight loss rate of 1 mg/°C. When 5% MPD-HPP-CQDs or 5% MPD-PA-CQDs were added into PVA, the Tmax1 were increased to be 344.8 and 312.9 °C for the two PVA-CQDs composite films with a decreasing decomposition rate of 0.50 mg/°C (Fig. 6b). Besides, the maximum decomposition temperature at second stage Tmax2 also increased to a higher value of 446.3 °C and 443.2 °C than that of pure PVA (428.5 °C). This was explained by the reason that the formed CQDs residual char protected the PVA matrix. At 800 °C, the residual char rates of pure PVA and MPD-CQDs-PVA were 4.51% and 5.49%, respectively, while the residual char content of 15% MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and 15% MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite film increased to 16.86% and 20.66% respectively. In terms of raw materials, the phosphorus content of HPP and PA was 14% and 28.2%, respectively. Therefore, it was concluded that the more phosphorus content caused a better flame retardant effect. Under air atmosphere, the residual char of PVA-CQDs composite films were also higher than pure PVA films. It was known that the decomposition process of PVA was affected by the involvement of oxygen. The addition of CQDs caused a slight decrease in Tmax1 of the PVA composite films, however, the Tmax2 were significantly increased. Furthermore, the glass transition temperature Tg were tested by DSC (Fig. S4). The results showed that Tg of PVA, 5% MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and 5% MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA was 79.1 °C, 78.9 °C, and 75.9 °C respectively. Obviously, all PVA composite films had only one Tg, indicating that the composite films were homogeneous. Compare with pure PVA film, the Tg for PVA-CQDs composite films decreased, which might be explained by the reason that the incorporation of CQDs would increase the free volume of polymer chains, and then promote their movement ability, consequently result in the lower Tg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardancy of PVA-CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Pure PVA film was easy to burn with a LOI value of 19.0%. Once it was ignited, the whole film could be burned out in 35 s without obvious residual char left. As shown in Table. S1, when 5%, 10%, and 15% of MPD-CQDs were added to PVA, the LOI values of the MPD-CQDs-PVA composite films were 19.8%, 20.3%, and 21% respectively. The UL-94 vertical burning tests of the three samples were shown in Fig. S4. It was found that the flame retardancy of MPD-CQDs-PVA gradually increased with the increasement of MPD-CQDs. As PVA was composited with 5% MPD-HPP-CQDs or 5%MPD-PA-CQDs (Fig. 7 ), the burning rate would decrease and the time for completely burning out was extended to above 60 s with an enhanced LOI above 21.0%, but the phenomenon of melting drops still occurred during the combustion process. When the adding content of CQDs increased, the PVA-CQDs composite films would appear self-extinguishing at both the first and second ignition with more remaining residual char. The UL-94 testing results showed that when 10% MPD-HPP-CQDs or 10% MPD-PA-CQDs was added, the LOI value of 10% MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and 10% MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA was 25.6% and 27.5%, and the UL-94 testing grade was VTM-1 and VTM-0 respectively. When the adding amount continued to increase, the LOI value was enhanced to be 27.8% and 31.5%, which made the PVA-CQDs composite films achieve non-inflammable level. From the above results, it was concluded that both MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs had good flame retardancy effect on PVA films. Due to a more phosphorus percentage and a higher LOI value, the MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite film with VTM-0 level could meet a higher requirement of material than MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA with VTM-1 level.

The MCC test results of PVA, MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films were shown in Fig. 8 and Table 3 . During the combustion process, two heat release temperature peaks was observed for PVA films at about 320 °C and 460 °C. The maximum peak of heat release rate (PHRR) for pure PVA was 289.3 and 35.8 W/g (Fig. 8a), and the total heat release (THR) was 21.3 kJ/g (Fig. 8b). The heat release curves of MPD-CQDs-PVA were similar to pure PVA, indicating that MPD-CQDs-PVA without phosphorus had little improvement on the heat release of PVA film. However, when PVA was composited with MPD-HPP-CQDs or MPD-PA-CQDs, their PHRR and THR greatly decreased, indicating an enhanced thermal stability and flame retardancy. It could be seen in Table 3 that when 10% MPD-PA-CQDs was added, the PHRR and THR of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA was 134.2 W/g and 15.0 KJ/g with a decrease of 53.6% and 29.6%. In general, the flame retardant effect of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA was better than MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA at a same adding amount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Residual char analysis of PVA-CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the carbon layer structure of PVA and composite film materials after combustion and carbonization was analyzed by observing the internal microstructure of the residual char. The SEM images of these residual chars were shown in Fig. 9 . The outer layer of the pure PVA residual char was loose and porous without a good physical barrier effect (Fig. 9a and a'). The smooth structure of the inner layer meant that most of the gas-phase products during combustion had escaped to the outer layer of the material, resulting in a large heat release, which was consistent with the above MCC data. Besides, the spread of flammable gases to the outer layer led to more serious combustion, which resulted in a violent burning. After adding CQDs to PVA, the structure of the char layer of PVA-CQDs changed significantly, as shown in Fig. 9 (b-d and b'-d'). The outer layer char became denser and the inner layer char appeared many bubbles with continuous closed vesicular structures, which indicated that the char layer had blocked the transfer of the released combustible gas, oxygen and heat, thus protecting the substrate from further oxidation and degradation. EDS characterizations (Fig. 9e, f) showed that the residual char after combustion contained nitrogen and phosphorus elements in the condensed phase. When the gas diffusion and oxygen transportation were hindered, the combustion of the films slowed down.

Raman spectroscopy was used to characterize the effect of CQDs on the degree of graphitization of residual char. As shown in Fig. 10 , the residual char of all samples exhibited two Raman absorption peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to disordered carbon (D) and graphitic carbon (G), respectively. In general, the degree of graphitization of the residual char could be evaluated by calculating the ratio of the integrated peak area of the D peak to the G peak (ID/IG). The smaller ID/IG values indicated a higher degree of graphitization. In pure PVA sample, the ID/IG value was 3.52, which was slightly reduced to 3.44 after the addition of phosphorus-free MPD-CQDs. When two phosphorus-containing CQDs, 5% MPD-HPP-CQDs and 5% MPD-PA-CQDs, were added to PVA, the ID/IG values were reduced to 3.23 and 3.04, respectively. It meant that the degree of graphitization of the residual char were greatly improved, representing a better barrier effect during the combustion process. In order to further understand the composition of the residual char, XPS spectroscopy including C1s, N1s, O1s and P2p were recorded (Fig. S6). For the C1s spectra of both MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA, the peak at 284.8 eV was attributed to C=C/C-C/C-H, the peak at 286.3 eV was attributed to C-O/C-N, and the peak at 289.4 eV was attributed to COOR. The two divided peaks in the N 1 s spectrum were NH (398.6 eV) and CN (400.6 eV). In the O1s spectrum of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA, there were two divided peaks at 533.6 eV and 531.7 eV, which was assigned as PO and C-O-C, respectively. In the P2p spectrum of MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA, the two peaks at 135.0 eV and 133.9 eV was belong to P-O-C and PO, respectively. In addition, two peaks at 133.4 eV and 134.5 eV were observed in the P2p spectrum of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA, corresponding to PN and PN, respectively. It was inferred that the char might contain polyphosphate, phosphorus nitride, and so on.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas product analysis of PVA-CQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: TG-FTIR was used to detect the gases generated during the thermal degradation of the samples, which helped to further understand the thermal degradation mechanism of PVA-CQDs composite films. According to the Lambert-Beer law, when the thermal cracking reaction occurred, the concentration of the resulting gaseous products could be linearly reflected by the absorbance intensity at a specific wave number. Typically, the height of the absorption peak indicated the concentration of gaseous products produced. Thus, the higher the height of the peak, the higher the concentration released. As shown in the 3D TG-FTIR (Fig. 11a) and the FTIR spectra of pyrolysis process at different temperature (Fig. 11b), almost no gas-phase product was generated below 180 °C for MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA, and the pyrolysis mainly occurred at 200–600 °C. At 300 °C, corresponding to the degradation of PVA chains and the breakage of some weak bonds in MPD-HPP-CQDs, the peaks appearing at 1300–1800 cm−1 included CO, CC, CC and CN bonds. Simultaneously the characteristic absorption peaks of P=O/C-O (1162 cm−1) and PO (1046 cm−1) appeared, indicating that the phosphorus-containing component on the MPD-HPP-CQDs began to be degraded. On the other hand, MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA showed the characteristic absorption peaks of NH3 (949 cm−1) except for the absorption peaks of CO, CC, CC, CN, P=O/C-O, and PO [34,35], implying that NH3 was released from MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA during the pyrolysis process and the release of NH3 might dilute the concentration of combustible gases and oxygen, and thus alleviate the degradation rate. It was inferred that the MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite film had better flame retardancy than MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA composite film, which was consistent with the results from UL-94 testing and LOI data.

In this work, two main phosphorus-containing CQDs made from m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and 2-carboxyl ethyl(phenyl)phosphinic acid (HPP)/phytanic acid (PA) were successfully synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. And then, the as-synthesized MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs were blended with PVA to prepare PVA-CQDs composite films via casting method. The fireproofing tests indicated that both MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs could enhance the flame retardancy of PVA films. However, because the percentage of phosphorus of PA (28.2%) was higher than that of HPP (14%), when adding the same amount of MPD-HPP-CQDs and MPD-PA-CQDs to PVA, the flame retardancy of MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA film was better than MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA film. It was concluded that P element played a crucial role in enhancing the flame retardancy of PVA. The intact layer of P-containing condensed phase of PVA matrix effectively hindered gas diffusion and oxygen transportation, resulting in a slower combustion rate and promoting further carbonation. Furthermore, the MPD-HPP-CQDs-PVA and MPD-PA-CQDs-PVA composite films exhibited excellent UV and blue-light shielding properties without affecting transparency of PVA itself, which provided a promising wide range of application prospects such as automotive films.

Runying Gao: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Xindan Yi: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xinyu Liu: Formal analysis, Methodology. Haojie Wang: Investigation. Lingzhi Wang: Formal analysis. Birong Zeng: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Guorong Chen: Formal analysis, Software. Yiting Xu: Methodology, Software. Conghui Yuan: Software, Methodology. Lizong Dai: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52173081, 52203339), and Central Government Guidance Local Science and Technology Development Funds in Fujian Province (2022L3002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.reactfunctpolym.2024.105877.

Hussen, Insights into optical band gap identification in polymer composite films based on PVA with enhanced optical properties: Structural and optical characteristics, Optical Materials, 133 (2022), 113007, doi: 10.1016/j.optmat.2022.113007.

ACS Nano

J. Appl. Polym. Sci.
